section.title: The Second Census
section.sectionRationale: After the plague at Peor closes out the old generation's story, Israel is numbered a second time on the plains of Moab, an entirely new generation ready to enter the land their parents were barred from. The chapter closes with the sober note that not one person counted here had also been counted forty years earlier, except Caleb and Joshua.
section.thesis: God commands a fresh census of the generation now standing ready to enter Canaan, tribe by tribe, so the land can be divided fairly by lot according to each tribe's size, and the record closes by confirming that the unbelieving generation of the first census has entirely died out in the wilderness as God had sworn.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: the command to number a new generation; the census of Reuben, Simeon, and Gad; Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun; Manasseh, Ephraim, and the daughters of Zelophehad; Benjamin, Dan, Asher, Naphtali, and the grand total; instructions for dividing the land by lot; and the Levites numbered separately, closing with the note that only Caleb and Joshua remained from the first census.

units.label: The command to number a new generation
units.note: twenty years of age or older

verses.text: After the plague had ended, the LORD said to Moses and Eleazar son of Aaron the priest,
“Take a census of the whole congregation of Israel by the houses of their fathers, all those twenty years of age or older who can serve in the army of Israel.”
So on the plains of Moab by the Jordan, across from Jericho, Moses and Eleazar the priest issued the instruction,
“Take a census of the men twenty years of age or older, as the LORD has commanded Moses.” And these were the Israelites who came out of the land of Egypt:

units.label: Reuben, Simeon, and Gad
units.note: the line of Korah did not die out

verses.text: Reuben was the firstborn of Israel. These were the descendants of Reuben: The Hanochite clan from Hanoch, the Palluite clan from Pallu,
the Hezronite clan from Hezron, and the Carmite clan from Carmi.
These were the clans of Reuben, and their {{registration|registration}} numbered 43,730.
Now the son of Pallu was Eliab,

and the sons of Eliab were Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram. It was Dathan and Abiram, chosen by the congregation, who fought against Moses and Aaron with the followers of Korah who rebelled against the LORD.
And the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them along with Korah, whose followers died when the fire consumed 250 men. They serve as a warning sign.
However, the line of Korah did not die out.
These were the descendants of Simeon by their clans: The Nemuelite clan from Nemuel, the Jaminite clan from Jamin, the Jachinite clan from Jachin,
the Zerahite clan from Zerah, and the Shaulite clan from Shaul.
These were the clans of Simeon, and there were 22,200 men.
These were the descendants of Gad by their clans: The Zephonite clan from Zephon, the Haggite clan from Haggi, the Shunite clan from Shuni,
the Oznite clan from Ozni, the Erite clan from Eri,
the Arodite clan from Arod, and the Arelite clan from Areli.
These were the clans of Gad, and their registration numbered 40,500.
The sons of Judah were Er and Onan, but they died in the land of Canaan.
These were the descendants of Judah by their clans: The Shelanite clan from Shelah, the Perezite clan from Perez, and the Zerahite clan from Zerah.
And these were the descendants of Perez: the Hezronite clan from Hezron and the Hamulite clan from Hamul.
These were the clans of Judah, and their registration numbered 76,500.
These were the descendants of Issachar by their clans: The Tolaite clan from Tola, the Punite clan from Puvah,
the Jashubite clan from Jashub, and the Shimronite clan from Shimron.
These were the clans of Issachar, and their registration numbered 64,300.
These were the descendants of Zebulun by their clans: The Seredite clan from Sered, the Elonite clan from Elon, and the Jahleelite clan from Jahleel.
These were the clans of Zebulun, and their registration numbered 60,500.
The descendants of Joseph included the clans of Manasseh and Ephraim.
These were the descendants of Manasseh: The Machirite clan from Machir, the father of Gilead, and the Gileadite clan from Gilead.
These were the descendants of Gilead: the Iezerite clan from Iezer, the Helekite clan from Helek,
the Asrielite clan from Asriel, the Shechemite clan from Shechem,
the Shemidaite clan from Shemida, and the Hepherite clan from Hepher.
Now Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons but only daughters. The names of his daughters were Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
These were the clans of Manasseh, and their registration numbered 52,700.
These were the descendants of Ephraim by their clans: The Shuthelahite clan from Shuthelah, the Becherite clan from Becher, and the Tahanite clan from Tahan.
And the descendants of Shuthelah were the Eranite clan from Eran.
These were the clans of Ephraim, and their registration numbered 32,500. These clans were the descendants of Joseph.

units.label: Benjamin, Dan, Asher, Naphtali, and the total

verses.text: These were the descendants of Benjamin by their clans: The Belaite clan from Bela, the Ashbelite clan from Ashbel, the Ahiramite clan from Ahiram,
the Shuphamite clan from Shupham, and the Huphamite clan from Hupham.
And the descendants of Bela from Ard and Naaman were the Ardite clan from Ard and the Naamite clan from Naaman.
These were the clans of Benjamin, and their registration numbered 45,600.
These were the descendants of Dan by their clans: The Shuhamite clan from Shuham. These were the clans of Dan.
All of them were Shuhamite clans, and their registration numbered 64,400.
These were the descendants of Asher by their clans: The Imnite clan from Imnah, the Ishvite clan from Ishvi, and the Beriite clan from Beriah.
And these were the descendants of Beriah: the Heberite clan from Heber and the Malchielite clan from Malchiel.
And the name of Asher's daughter was Serah.
These were the clans of Asher, and their registration numbered 53,400.
These were the descendants of Naphtali by their clans: The Jahzeelite clan from Jahzeel, the Gunite clan from Guni,
the Jezerite clan from Jezer, and the Shillemite clan from Shillem.
These were the clans of Naphtali, and their registration numbered 45,400.
These men of Israel numbered 601,730 in all.

units.label: The land to be divided by lot
units.note: according to the number of names

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“The land is to be divided among the tribes as an {{inheritance|inheritance}}, according to the number of names.
Increase the inheritance for a large tribe and decrease it for a small one; each tribe is to receive its inheritance according to the number of those registered.
Indeed, the land must be divided by lot; they shall receive their inheritance according to the names of the tribes of their fathers.
Each inheritance is to be divided by lot among the larger and smaller tribes.”

units.label: The Levites, and the passing of the old generation
units.note: not one was left except Caleb and Joshua

verses.text: Now these were the Levites numbered by their clans: The Gershonite clan from Gershon, the Kohathite clan from Kohath, and the Merarite clan from Merari.
These were the families of the Levites: The Libnite clan, the Hebronite clan, the Mahlite clan, the Mushite clan, and the Korahite clan. Now Kohath was the father of Amram,
and Amram's wife was named Jochebed. She was also a daughter of Levi, born to Levi in Egypt. To Amram she bore Aaron, Moses, and their sister Miriam.
Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar were born to Aaron,
but Nadab and Abihu died when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD.
The registration of the Levites totaled 23,000, every male a month old or more; they were not numbered among the other Israelites, because no inheritance was given to them among the Israelites.
These were the ones numbered by Moses and Eleazar the priest when they counted the Israelites on the plains of Moab by the Jordan, across from Jericho.
Among all these, however, there was not one who had been numbered by Moses and Aaron the priest when they counted the Israelites in the Wilderness of Sinai.
For the LORD had told them that they would surely die in the wilderness. Not one was left except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

registration.gloss: To number, to muster, to appoint. The same root gives Israel's census its name, and it carries a sense of God's oversight and accounting for His people, not merely a bureaucratic headcount but a marking of who stands ready under His hand.

inheritance.gloss: Inheritance, a possession passed down. The land is not earned by conquest alone but given as nachalah, a gift tied to family and tribe, distributed by lot so that human favoritism cannot determine who receives what; God's own hand decides the outcome.

basic.context: After the plague at Peor finally ends, God commands a second census, this time of an entirely new generation standing ready on the plains of Moab to enter the promised land. The chapter carefully lists tribe after tribe, then closes with a sobering note: of everyone counted forty years earlier, only Caleb and Joshua remain.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real administrative record with real theological weight. The long lists of clans and numbers are not filler; they document, in the most concrete way possible, that God kept His word both to judge the unbelieving generation and to preserve and multiply a new one ready to receive the promise.
basic.originalLanguages: The census uses paqad, to number or muster, the same word that gives the book of Numbers its Hebrew shape, portraying God's people as accounted for under His watchful care. The land itself is to be given as nachalah, inheritance, distributed not by human preference but by lot, so that the outcome rests entirely in God's hand.
basic.typology: That not one person from the first census remained, except the two faithful spies, pictures the truth that unbelief truly excludes people from the promise while faith truly inherits it, a pattern the New Testament applies directly to the church's own need to persevere in faith rather than presume upon past privilege.
basic.christ: Hebrews 3 and 4 use this very generation's failure to enter God's rest as a warning and an invitation: just as an entire unbelieving generation died in the wilderness while a new generation of faith entered the land, so all who hold fast their confidence in Christ enter the greater rest He provides, while unbelief still excludes.
basic.god: God is faithful both to His judgments and to His promises in the very same chapter: the old generation dies exactly as He swore, yet the covenant promise to Abraham's offspring continues unbroken in the new generation now numbered and ready to receive the land.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 3:16-19 draws directly on this wilderness generation's unbelief as a warning against hardened hearts. Numbers 14:29-30 records the original oath that only Caleb and Joshua of that generation would enter the land. Joshua 14:6-15 later records Caleb's actual entry and inheritance, fulfilling the promise made decades earlier.
basic.oneStory: An entire generation's unbelief truly cost them the promise, while a new generation, numbered here on the very edge of Canaan, stands ready to receive what their parents forfeited. This pattern of judgment on unbelief and preserved promise for faith runs through the whole Bible, warning and inviting every generation to hold fast its confidence in God to the end.

context.literary: This second census mirrors the first census in Numbers 1 in structure and purpose, but its content marks an entirely new cast of names, underscoring that forty years and an entire generation have passed between the two counts.
context.historical: Conducted on the plains of Moab just before entering Canaan, this census served the practical purpose of allotting land fairly by tribe size, while also functioning as Israel's official transition from a wilderness-wandering people to a nation on the verge of settlement.
context.canonical: This census and its closing note become a key text behind the New Testament's warnings about persevering faith, especially in Hebrews, where the wilderness generation's exclusion from God's rest becomes a sober pattern applied to believers today.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To document, with careful, verifiable detail, that God's judgment on the unbelieving first generation was total and that His promise to their descendants remained fully intact, preparing a numbered, organized people for the conquest and settlement of the land.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter is historical record and covenant bookkeeping; it does not prescribe a census method for later communities. Its abiding principle is that God's word of judgment and God's word of promise are both kept perfectly, even across a full generation's passing.

debates.question: Why do the numbers in this census differ so little in total from the first census forty years earlier, despite the deaths of an entire generation?

views.label: God's ongoing blessing of fruitfulness even in judgment
views.case: Even under the sentence of wandering, God continued to bless Israel with children, so that new generations replaced the old and the nation's overall size remained roughly stable despite the loss of everyone twenty and older from the first census.

views.label: The numbers reflect stylized, rounded figures typical of ancient record-keeping
views.case: Some scholars note that census totals in the ancient world often used round or symbolic figures for administrative and rhetorical purposes, meaning the numbers communicate scale and completeness rather than a precise modern head count.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the chapter's central point: God's covenant faithfulness to multiply Abraham's descendants continued unbroken even through severe judgment on a specific generation's unbelief, and the text intends for readers to trust God's demonstrated pattern of both judgment and blessing.

study.originalLanguages: The census, paqad, mustering God's people clan by clan, functions as a careful accounting under God's own watchful oversight. The land to come is nachalah, inheritance, allotted by lot rather than human choice, a structure meant to keep the distribution of God's gift free from favoritism or manipulation.

typology.type: An entire unbelieving generation excluded from the land while a new generation of faith enters
typology.fulfillment: Applied directly in Hebrews 3:16-19 and 4:1-2 as a warning that unbelief excludes from God's rest, while those who hold fast their confidence in Christ enter it.

typology.type: Caleb and Joshua alone surviving from the first census
typology.fulfillment: A picture of the remnant of faith preserved through judgment, anticipating the New Testament's assurance that God always preserves a faithful remnant for Himself.

study.christ: Hebrews 3 and 4 take this wilderness generation's exclusion from the land as their central Old Testament illustration for urging believers to persevere in faith: just as unbelief kept an entire generation from entering Canaan while Caleb and Joshua's faith carried them through, so today all who hold their confidence in Christ firm to the end enter the greater and final rest He secures, while hardened unbelief still excludes.
study.god: God keeps both His hard word and His kind word with equal precision: the sentence of death on the unbelieving generation is carried out to the last person, while His covenant promise to multiply and preserve Abraham's offspring continues unbroken in the new generation numbered here on the edge of the land.

crossReferences.note: Uses this generation's unbelief as a direct warning against hardening one's heart against God.
Records the original oath that only Caleb and Joshua of the first census generation would enter the land, fulfilled exactly here.
Records Caleb's actual entry into and inheritance of the land decades after this promise was first made.
Warns believers to fear falling short of God's rest as this generation did, urging faith united with the promise heard.

study.oneStory: Judgment on unbelief and preservation of promise sit side by side in this single census: an entire generation dies exactly as God swore, while a new generation stands ready, numbered and organized, to receive what their parents forfeited by unbelief. This pattern, that faith inherits the promise while unbelief truly excludes, runs through the whole Bible and lands, in Hebrews, as a direct warning and invitation to hold fast to Christ to the very end.

quiz.q: What was the immediate occasion for this second census, according to verse 1?

options.text: It was taken after the plague at Peor had ended.
options.note: Verse 1 ties the census directly to the end of that plague.

options.text: It was taken immediately after leaving Egypt.
options.note: That was the occasion for the first census in Numbers 1, forty years earlier, not this one.

options.text: It was taken before crossing the Red Sea.
options.note: This census occurs on the plains of Moab, near the end of the wilderness period, not near the Red Sea crossing.

options.text: No occasion is given for the census.
options.note: Verse 1 states the occasion explicitly.

quiz.q: According to verses 9 to 11, what happened to Korah's line despite his rebellion?

options.text: Korah himself was swallowed by the earth, but his line did not die out.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 state this distinction precisely.

options.text: His entire line, including all descendants, was wiped out completely.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly states the line did not die out, despite Korah's own judgment.

options.text: His descendants were made priests as a reward.
options.note: No such reward is mentioned; the text simply notes his line's survival despite his rebellion.

options.text: The text says nothing further about Korah's descendants.
options.note: Verse 11 addresses this directly.

quiz.q: What was the total number of Israelite men counted in this census, according to verse 51?

options.note: Verse 51 states this exact total.
That was the total from the first census in Numbers 1, not this second one.
That figure refers only to the Levites, counted separately in verse 62, not the total of all Israel.
Verse 51 gives the exact total.

quiz.q: How was the land to be divided among the tribes, according to verses 52 to 56?

options.text: By lot, with larger tribes receiving proportionally more according to their registered numbers.
options.note: Verses 54 to 56 describe this exact method.

options.text: Equally among all twelve tribes regardless of size.
options.note: The text specifies proportional allotment by size, not equal division.

options.text: By each tribe's military strength in battle.
options.note: The method described is casting lots based on registered population, not battle prowess.

options.text: No method for dividing the land is given.
options.note: Verses 52 to 56 describe the method in detail.

quiz.q: According to verses 63 to 65, who was left from those counted in the first census forty years earlier?

options.text: Only Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.
options.note: Verse 65 states this precisely, fulfilling the LORD's earlier oath.

options.text: All of the original tribal leaders remained.
options.note: The text specifies that only Caleb and Joshua remained, not the tribal leaders generally.

options.text: Only the Levites survived from the first census.
options.note: The Levites are counted separately, but the text names only Caleb and Joshua as survivors of the wilderness judgment.

options.text: No one from the first census survived, including Caleb and Joshua.
options.note: Verse 65 explicitly names them as the exceptions.

questions: An entire generation's unbelief truly cost them entry into the promise, while a new generation stood ready to receive it. Where might unbelief be quietly costing you something God intends for you to receive by faith?

Caleb and Joshua alone survived from the first census, having trusted God when the rest of their generation did not. What would it look like for you to hold a minority position of faith even when most around you do not share it?

God's judgment on the old generation and His faithfulness to multiply the new generation appear in the very same chapter. How do you hold together God's justice and God's kindness in your own understanding of Him?

The land was to be divided by lot, not by human favoritism, so that God's own hand determined the outcome. Where do you need to trust God's ordering of your circumstances rather than striving to control the outcome yourself?

Korah's judgment was severe, yet his line did not die out entirely. Where do you need hope that God's mercy can preserve something good even out of a season of real failure or judgment in your own life?

translationNotes.note: The parenthetical note about Dathan and Abiram interrupts the genealogical list to recall Korah's rebellion from Numbers 16, showing the author deliberately weaving warning into the record of a new generation's hope.
Zelophehad's daughters are named here in the middle of a genealogical list, preparing the reader for their formal legal case in the very next chapter, Numbers 27.

The phrase not one was left except Caleb and Joshua fulfills the specific oath recorded in Numbers 14:29-30, and the repetition across two widely separated chapters underscores the narrator's intent to show God's word kept precisely over four decades.
